original_abstract: This paper introduces the Network Data Repository (NR), the first comprehensive web-based platform designed for interactive graph analytics and visualization. While traditional repositories serve as static archives for downloading datasets, NR provides a dynamic ecosystem where users can analyze, explore, and visualize network data in real-time. Currently hosting over 500 graphs across 19 diverse domains—including social, biological, and technological networks—the platform enables researchers to compare global graph statistics, examine node-level features, and investigate structural connectivity through an intuitive, multi-dimensional interface. By providing tools for filtering, scaling, and zooming, NR moves beyond the limitations of static plots, allowing for the discovery of complex patterns and the verification of research claims with minimal effort. Furthermore, NR fosters scientific collaboration by integrating social features that allow the community to share visualizations, discuss findings, and contribute new datasets. Through its fusion of a large-scale data archive with powerful analytical tools, NR aims to accelerate the study of network science and improve the reproducibility of graph-based research.
humanized_abstract: Graph algorithms and structures are ubiquitous in numerous disciplines of science and engineering, and many research contributions are built on experimenting and analyzing numerous graph datasets. Although there exist many datasets, current practice is that researchers download their favorite datasets and experiment with them in a local environment. Data sharing repositories exist but mainly serve as downloadable storage for data and do not offer a platform for in situ experimentation. Motivated by the needs and practices of the network science community, we introduce the Network Data Repository (NR), the first data repository specifically designed for network data sharing with the functionality of interactive graph analytics and visualization. While current data sharing practices treat data repositories as a collection of downloadable files, NR transforms the repository into a working environment allowing for interactive graph exploration, experimentation, and visualization. The NR features a collection of 500 graphs distributed in 19 datasets across 19 diverse domains such as social networks, biological networks, and technological networks, and provides researchers with the means to compare graph properties and quantities at multiple levels, inspect node characteristics and neighborhood structures, and to browse and search the connectivity pattern between nodes and their properties through an interactive, multi-dimensional graph visualization system. Furthermore, the NR facilitates feature sharing through social media, graph visualization sharing, discussion and more. Hence, the NR is expected to facilitate rapid progress in network science, as well as improve the reproducibility of graph-related results and theories.
